title: “My Mother Turned Down A Record Deal With Ice Cube in 1992”: An Interview With Seth Neblett, Author of ‘Mothership Connected: The Women of Parliament-Funkadelic’
author: Jack Riedy
summary:

Parliament-Funkadelic is an essential ingredient in American popular music of the past six decades. The new book Mothership Connected
full_text:
Art via Evan Solano

Show your love of the game by subscribing to Passion of the Weiss on Patreon so that we can keep churning out interviews with legendary producers, feature the best emerging rap talent in the game, and gift you the only worthwhile playlists left in this streaming hellscape.

You can find Jack Riedy’s upcoming DJ sets on his Instagram.

Parliament-Funkadelic is an essential ingredient in American popular music of the past six decades. The new book Mothership Connected tells the story of peak P-Funk from the perspective of the female members, long unsung but integral to the sound of the band.

The book follows five women who joined the traveling funk ensemble led by George Clinton and eventually their own sub-groups: Brides of Funkenstein’s Dawn Silva and Lynn Mabry, and Parlet members Debbie Wright, Shirley Hayden, and Mallia Franklin. In addition to her powerhouse vocals, Franklin was an ersatz manager and talent scout who hosted the band at her Detroit home in their early days and introduced pivotal players Bootsy Collins, guitarist Catfish Collins, and keyboardist/arranger Junie Morrison to the group. These women contributed to classics like “Flash Light”, “Cosmic Slop”, “(Not Just) Knee Deep”, and heralded the Mothership across the country on tour.

VIDEO

Each singer has stories of rock star hedonism; it was common for P-funkers to be paid a baggie of cocaine per show. At times Clinton’s leadership makes the band seem closer to a cult or a narcotics enterprise. There are plenty of intra-band hookups and relationships that sometimes curdle into physical and emotional abuse. Parliament and Funkadelic were formally disbanded in the early ‘80s amidst drug problems and label conflict. Clinton continued his career with a rotating cast of players and band names, and P-Funk’s former vocalists found work across the music industry with Talking Heads, Ice Cube, Snoop Dogg, and beyond.

As the legacy of the band grew through aesthetic influence and widespread sampling, the women of P-Funk were continually disregarded in favor of their male compatriots. In 1997, the Rock n Roll Hall of Fame inducted Parliament-Funkadelic but did not honor any female members. In 2006, music journal Wax Poetics devoted an issue to the group and interviewed ten key figures, all men.

Mothership Connected corrects these omissions. The oral history format allows the five principals, and others like Clinton, Bootsy, and Sly Stone, to present their differing perspectives and mismatched memories. The project originally began as a documentary film, with interviews gathered between 2007 and 2021.

As Mallia Franklin’s only child, author Seth Neblett grew up around the group. In the book’s prologue, he recalls his mother changing into her “ordinary” clothes after one stop of the 1978 Flash Light Tour: “For P-Funk, ordinary was not ordinary at all, just a bit less outrageous. With burgundy platform boots, feathers in her hair, and a beige-and-white mink coat with a fluffy white fox collar, we hit the corridors of the arena.“ Neblett leveraged his history with the funk family to elicit stories of great vulnerability from his subjects, many of whom have since passed away, including Wright and Franklin. The book illuminates key players of the P-Funk legacy, and it’s also a touching tribute from a son to his mother.

I spoke to Seth Neblett last fall about documenting a musical empire, interviewing his extended funk family, and going to a Prince show with Apollonia.

​​(This interview has been condensed and lightly edited for clarity.)

What did you first think of your mom’s work when you were growing up?

Seth Neblett: The prelude of the book is very accurate as far as my first recollection of what my mother did. My mother was going to a studio, and I knew my mother was doing music, but I didn’t really understand it until the night I went to Cobo Arena, January 1st, 1978. As a little six-year-old going to this massive arena and seeing these thousands of people.

I’ve only experienced screams like that one other time in my life, when I went to see Prince on the Purple Rain tour. The screams were so loud that it’s almost like you have to cover your ears because it’s so permeating through your body. So, yeah, that was my first introduction to what she did. And then not long after that, I remember riding home from school and passing a neighborhood record store. The whole front, these big posters of Parlet, me seeing it and making my grandmother stop. Going into this record store, all these posters, and your mother is right there. It was so surreal. And I remember my grandmother actually asking the staff, ‘could I have a poster?’, her explaining to them that was my mother in the middle, them giving her a promotional poster, and my grandmother putting it on my bedroom wall.

That’s sweet. So you had seen the big Mothership experience live. Were you listening to her albums at home at all? Were you a little too young for that at the time?

Seth Neblett: You know what was really interesting was that I listened to the music. I remember hearing the music when they were demoed, a lot of the best stuff. When they were recording the first album, Pleasure Principle, Mom would come home with demos. My first real remembrance of the albums was, there was a little grocery store that was around the corner from our house, and at the checkout counter, there was a wire rack of albums. If I had a little money and I saw a Parliament album, I would buy it because in my mind, this is what I should do. So I was very young, but I knew the music.

Let’s say from 1978 to 1981 or 1982, you know, she took me to the studio. So there were many sessions, of all those songs, that I witnessed.

What was that experience like? What age were you when you were going along to those sessions?

Seth Neblett: 10, 11. And when you get into the ‘80s, you’re talking about George Clinton’s solo stuff. My mom would take me to the studio and they’re going over the same line over and over and over. Back then, I had no appreciation for it. It was like it was a chore.

What other music did you get into as you were growing up? Was your mom a big influence or did you diverge into your own taste?

Seth Neblett: When I was young, I was a big KISS fan. I was very into “Love Gun” and “Dynasty” and all of that stuff. And then I discovered Prince. And when I say discovered Prince, I mean discovered “I Wanna Be Your Lover”, Farrah Fawcett flip, the early stuff. In a way I introduced my mother to Prince. I had this elderly Italian babysitter, I’ll never forget her name was Francis, and she would take me to department stores and things like that. Well, I got Francis to buy me the Prince Dirty Mind album in 1980. And of course she didn’t understand what it was. So she bought it, and I remember taking it home.

VIDEO

The real raunchy stuff.

Seth Neblett: Yeah, I went from Parliament and Kiss into the whole Prince, Time, Vanity 6, Apollonia 6, Sheila E. And it felt like a natural progression, you know? The Prince empire, in a lot of ways, was like the Clinton empire.

If you were a fan early enough with Dirty Mind, then by the time he’s becoming a superstar, you’ve been a fan for years.

Seth Neblett: Oh, yeah. I had all the albums. I had Controversy, I had 1999. Living in Detroit, we had a very famous disc jockey here by the name of the Electrifying Mojo. And the Electrifying Mojo was one of those DJs that broke a lot of artists on black radio here in Michigan, and that spilled over into Ohio. And he had his whole shtick. He also did that for Prince. He broke The Time here, Vanity 6 here. He’d play the Prince album cuts, like “Private Joy” and “All the Critics Love You in New York.” Anytime Prince was in town, Prince would do radio interviews with Mojo. It was kind of a big deal.

What I didn’t understand was that Prince was a big fan of Parliament-Funkadelic. And so my mother had this surface relationship with Prince. Around the Purple Rain era, he hired a lot of Parliament-Funkadelic’s former staff. Billy Sparks, who’s in Purple Rain as the club manager, used to work for Parliament-Funkadelic. Wally Seyford and Greg Brooks, who were Prince’s security in the Purple Rain era and became members of the band by Sign o the Times, were both former members of the security team for P-Funk.

VIDEO

I knew he was a fan, but I didn’t know the connections went that deep.

Seth Neblett: The first time my mother met Prince, he was on tour opening up for Rick James. Rick and my mother had been friends for a few years. And there’s actually a story about Rick James getting his record deal in my book. I remember going to the concert when Prince was the opening act.

It’s interesting thinking about the Midwest connections, in particular with Prince and P-Funk.

Seth Neblett: My mother and another one of the Parlet members, Debbie Wright, became acquainted and started working with Parliament-Funkadelic and George Clinton very early, in the early ‘70s. Actually, they met him in the late ‘60s, but they started recording with George in the early ‘70s. Detroit was home base for Parliament- Funkadelic, and around ‘73 or ‘74, my mother left Detroit and went to Los Angeles with her sister, Jennifer Franklin, and Jennifer’s fiance, who was a Motown producer by the name of Jeffrey Bowen.

A year or so later, Parliament-Funkadelic released Mothership Connection, and they became a really big group. My mother continued to record with Parliament-Funkadelic because when George would come out to LA to record, he would always call her down to the studio. She recorded on all the Parliament albums: Up for the Down Stroke, Chocolate City, Mothership Connection.

When it was time to do the Mothership Connection tour, she was in LA trying to fulfill her dream as a solo artist, so George enlisted Debbie Wright to go on the road for the tour, and Debbie brought a girl that she knew, a teenager by the name of Jeanette Washington. Debbie and Jeanette became the first official female members of Parliament-Funkadelic as far as touring and photographed as members.

One of the opening acts on the Mothership Connection tour was Sly Stone. And Sly was, I don’t want to say on the downslide, but he was kind of coasting off of those old Sly and the Family Stone hits. And he had a trio of background girls, Lynn Mabry, Dawn Silva, and Virginia Ayers. George took an instant liking to Sly’s background singers. Sly’s background girls started hanging out with Parliament-Funkadelic, and Sly did not like that. So Sly fired them, and George saw an opportunity and said ‘you can go on tour with us right now.’ That move created tension between the original girls, and the new girls Lynn and Dawn.

VIDEO

At this point there are no Brides, there are no Parlets, they’re all background singers. When George finally decides that he’s going to split these girls off and get record deals for them, that’s when he wanted to bring my mother back into the fold. Originally, according to Dawn Silva, his original thought was that Mallia Franklin would come in and be the third Bride of Funkenstein. In the book, my mother talks about how she came into this war that was going on between these Detroit girls and these California girls. George wasn’t sure where Mallia would fit in, and she ended up being a Parlet with Casablanca Records.

It really created an environment of competition that lasted through the whole duration of these two groups, feeling like they had to compete with each other. Feeling like George may have done more for this group and not this group, really paying a lot of attention to the Brides and Parlet falling by the wayside – I think, personally, because my mother was being very vocal about not getting paid correctly, and that was creating an issue for the organization because she, unlike the other women and even some of the guys, was very outspoken and she was chirping up about the fact that she was not getting paid.

P-Funk guitarist Garry Shider, who wore the infamous diaper, gave a very telling quote in the book where he said that one of the ways that the organization kept control of the members was they always had your replacement on standby. That was one reason why P-Funk had five guitar players and four bass players and three drummers and ten singers. ‘If you don’t want to play ball, don’t worry, your replacement is already here.’ And that created a huge sense of competition, not between just the women, but also the men, because you were always paranoid about your gig. I think that women, especially women of color historically, unfortunately have been trained to compete against each other.

I appreciate that the book has a lot of different perspectives. Beyond all the women, you have quotes from people like Sly and George and the other members of the band to fill in some of that background. Can you talk to me about the process of speaking to all these people?

Seth Neblett: I interviewed George early on and was very honest with him. I don’t think that anything that anybody said was necessarily surprising to him. I said ‘okay, Uncle George, you know there are going to be people that are going to say that this is the greatest thing that ever happened to them, and then there are going to be people that are going to say that being a part of the group was the worst mistake they ever made.’ That’s why it was so important for me to have him speak on whatever he wanted to comment on.

The main reason that I wanted this to be an oral history is that even though I’m the writer, this is not about me. I wanted to give a broad view. George Clinton, Bootsy Collins, Bernie Worrell, Sly Stone, Garry Shider, Ron Dunbar. It was also important to include the business people, because they give you a totally different perspective. They were dealing with the IRS and the record companies. I included the road managers like Cheryl James and Ron Brimbrey, and people that ran the offices, like Leslie Fasino and Brenda Pierce. It was important for me to do that because the story of Parliament-Funkadelic is probably a thousand stories.

It does feel very epic.

Seth Neblett: What I tried to focus on is the stories of my mother, the stories of the women, and how these other participants saw the story of the women of Parliament-Funkadelic. But what I think came out of it was really the story of Parliament-Funkadelic.

You can’t really separate them in that way.

Seth Neblett: I think what I did in my book is something that many readers and fans didn’t get in George’s memoir. How could something so powerful just disintegrate into nothing, in the matter of a year or two? Because 75% of it is those Mothership years, I think my book is a pretty accurate depiction of what those last days were like. Including the voices of these business people was so important because I think they gave the reader a complete view of what was going on in the ending of the organization, the IRS coming or the drug dealers showing up and pulling guns on the secretaries. Mismanagement of money and the influx of drugs and those sorts of things really destroyed a musical empire.

That’s a great way to put it. It does have this tragic scope to it, especially when you have so many people offering their perspectives on it by that point in the book.

Seth Neblett: The mothership ended up in a junkyard.

There are some really dark stories that emerge from people’s pasts. As you described, there’s drugs that come into play and other tough stories that I’m sure were difficult for them to recount. How did you handle those more sensitive stories?

Seth Neblett: I think that I had a leg up because I knew these people, and they had known me since I was a child. So I came in with an all-access pass, so to speak. I think being forthcoming and vulnerable with me was a lot easier because they weren’t talking to a stranger, they were talking to a family member. Because I came in with that legitimacy, it was a lot easier for these women to bare their souls and be honest about their experiences in it. Having these relationships with these people pretty much all my life, they knew that they could be honest with me.

It takes me back to something that Bootsy Collins, my godfather, said to me when this whole process started. I didn’t plan to be the writer of this thing, and I wrote that in the book. When these women and I first started talking about writing a book, there were other writers in the mix. We just couldn’t find the right fit. I remember moving to Los Angeles in 2006, and me and my mother had dinner with Bootsy, and I complained that we couldn’t find the right people, and Bootsy literally turned to me and said ‘you do it.’ I looked at him and I said, ‘what do you mean I do it? I don’t know how to do it.’ And he said, no, you know how to do it, you do it.’ Once he said that, I just decided I was going to do it. Some things in your life you’re just destined to do.

I think especially when my mother died in 2010, I made it a priority. I had to make it a destiny because I had to make sure her story was told, and then there was a group of women that stood by my side and said ‘we want you to see this through’. They were willing to tell their stories, and some of them very intimate stories about abuse and violence. Those things were important because I wanted to define who these women were when they walked through the door of Parliament-Funkadelic. If the reader understood some of these traumas these women had gone through, maybe they would understand some of the decisions they made as members of P-Funk.

VIDEO

By getting so much of their backstory, you understand them each as individuals and how they work together or don’t. You really get a sense of each woman’s personality in the book.

Seth Neblett: Yes, and that was very important for me because this was also a book about women’s studies. This was really a study in their relationships with each other, their relationships with men, their relationships with each other because of men. I think those things were very important to this story because those things are very important in life.

Was there anything that really surprised you in interviews?

Seth Neblett: The story that surprised me more than anything else was when I found out from Bruce Peterson, a former manager of the band who also managed my mother in her solo career, that she turned down a record deal with Ice Cube in 1992. I had never heard that story ever in my life. What do you mean she turned down a record deal with Sylvia Rhone and Ice Cube, and Dr. Dre was going to produce, and George was going to produce, and Prince was going to give her songs…I was flabbergasted. That was probably the most shocking story.

I think that also speaks to your mother’s contributions in a way, right? She was a musical talent, but also had this talent for making connections and networking and bringing people into the fold.

Seth Neblett: Yes, George called her the Geiger counter. During my interviews, he said she was the best talent scout that funk music ever had.

It is so crazy to read this and understand how small the world is. I didn’t realize that Lynn Mabry is Sly Stone’s cousin.

Seth Neblett: Her mother, Latanya, was Sly’s first cousin. There’s so many connections in this whole thing. Lynn and Sly, my mother and George’s fiance, Liz Bishop, getting Rick James a record deal, my mother being engaged to James Brown in the ‘80s, somebody who was her idol in the ‘60s, and Bootsy coming from James Brown’s band. The web is really small.

Someone asked me not long ago how difficult it was to reach these people for interviews. If I didn’t have the number myself, all I usually had to do was call one person and I could find the person.

I noticed that your press photo is credited to Apollonia. Is that the Apollonia?

Seth Neblett: Yes, Apollonia from Purple Rain. I do a podcast with her called Apollonia Studio 6. We have two seasons thus far.

VIDEO

I will have to get caught up. How did you strike up a friendship with her?

Seth Neblett: I met Apollonia through a mutual friend of ours who was one of Prince’s singers in the ‘80s, a woman by the name of Jill Jones. Jill and I had been friends for a few years, and of course Jill had maintained a relationship with Apollonia after Purple Rain. When Vanity died, Jill and I were going to drive from Los Angeles up to San Jose to go to her memorial. And Apollonia just said ‘If you don’t mind, I’ll ride with you.’ That trip turned into such a monumental, unbelievable trip. So I went and picked up Apollonia and, you know, me and her, from that first moment we met, we were locked in. It was just like we had known each other forever.

We went up to Denise’s memorial, and Apollonia was reunited with Susan and Brenda of Vanity 6, she hadn’t seen them since Purple Rain. The craziest thing was that the next night in Oakland, Prince announced that he was doing a Piano and a Microphone show. He had heard through the grapevine, because he has his spies everywhere, that Apollonia 6 and Jill were at Vanity’s memorial, and he invited them to his show. And so myself, Apollonia, Jill, Susan Moonsie of Vanity/Apollonia 6, we all went to the Piano and a Microphone show. We had been informed by his manager that he was having a special party in San Francisco, so we went. Even though he and my mother had been friends for decades, my mother was in Graffiti Bridge as a matter of fact, I got a chance to meet him myself, and that was beautiful. Once he realized I was Mallia Franklin’s son, he just opened up to me. And within 60 days, he was gone. It was unbelievable.

But through all of that, you know, myself and Apollonia became this yin and yang with each other. We’ve had different people on the podcast, we’ve had actors like Tia Carrere was on, we’ve had comedians like Jo Koy and Gabriel Iglesias, and then a lot of the Prince people like Jill Jones, Andre Cymone, Anna Fantastic, Susannah Melvoin.

Actually one of my proudest moments thus far was I conducted a surprise for Apollonia, and I reunited Apollonia 6. We turned it into a three-part episode situation. That was the first time they had done an interview together since Purple Rain. Yes, Apollonia Kotero took my author’s photo.

I just want to convey the importance of these women’s stories. They have been overlooked for 45, almost 50 years. They were overlooked in the inductions of the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, the Grammys. More than anything else I want to cement my mother’s legacy. If my mother had never brought Bootsy Collins, brought Junie Morrison who produced “One Nation Under a Groove” and “(Not Just) Knee Deep” and “Aqua Boogie,” if she never brought in David Lee Spradley who did the music for “Atomic Dog,” if she never stood in the studio when they were recording “Atomic Dog” and started panting like a dog, if she had never introduced Dr. Dre to Roger Troutman…outside of being a singer, she made moves that changed popular music. Because she was a woman, because she wasn’t famous, she wasn’t acknowledged. And I’m here to right that wrong. I think what this book did for me was maybe learn to appreciate her more.

VIDEO
